agent_output: {"executive_summary":"This architecture has three critical integration points with distinct failure characteristics. The synchronous chain from Order→Payment→Stripe creates a cascading failure risk where any downstream outage blocks order processing entirely. The fire-and-forget notification call silently loses emails with no recovery path. The design needs circuit breakers, retries with idempotency, a transactional outbox for notifications, and timeout budgets to prevent cascade amplification.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Accepts customer orders, orchestrates payment charging, triggers notifications","boundaries":"Owns order lifecycle state (created, payment_pending, paid, failed). Does NOT own payment state or notification delivery state."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Abstracts payment gateway interactions, manages payment records and idempotency keys","boundaries":"Owns payment attempt records and idempotency. Translates gateway-specific responses into domain payment events."},{"name":"Stripe Gateway","responsibility":"Third-party payment processing","boundaries":"External system outside our control. SLA is best-effort (~99.95% availability). Rate limits, maintenance windows, and degraded modes are unpredictable."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sends confirmation emails to customers","boundaries":"Owns delivery channel logic (email templates, provider selection). Does NOT own the decision of when to notify — that belongs to Order Service."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP POST /payments with order_id, amount, currency, idempotency_key. Returns payment_id, status (succeeded|failed|pending).","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral (Order blocks until Payment responds; Order's availability ≤ Payment's availability)"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Stripe Gateway","contract":"Synchronous HTTPS to Stripe Charges API with idempotency key. Returns charge result.","coupling_type":"temporal + platform (Payment blocks on external third-party; subject to Stripe's rate limits, TLS negotiation latency, and maintenance windows)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Fire-and-forget HTTP POST /notifications with order details. No response handling.","coupling_type":"fire-and-forget (no temporal coupling, but zero delivery guarantee — data loss on any failure)"}],"data_flow":"Customer → Order Service → [sync] Payment Service → [sync] Stripe → response propagates back → Order Service → [async fire-and-forget] Notification Service → Email Provider","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Synchronous Request-Response Chain","rationale":"Current design for payment flow — provides immediate consistency but creates tight temporal coupling"},{"pattern":"Fire-and-Forget","rationale":"Current design for notifications — decouples latency but sacrifices delivery guarantees"}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Ordering","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root): id, items, total, status, payment_reference, idempotency_key"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["Payment (root): id, order_id, amount, status, gateway_reference, idempotency_key, attempts[]"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root): id, order_id, channel, status, retry_count, last_attempted_at"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Ordering","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Payment Service defines the payment result contract; Order Service conforms to it)"},{"upstream":"Ordering","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Order Service publishes notification triggers; Notification Service conforms)"},{"upstream":"Stripe Gateway","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Conformist (Payment Service must conform to Stripe's API; we add an Anti-Corruption Layer to translate)"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Keep Order→Payment synchronous rather than go fully async","gains":"Immediate consistency — customer gets a definitive paid/failed response in the same request. Simpler programming model. No eventual consistency edge cases to explain to users.","sacrifices":"Order Service availability is bounded by Payment Service × Stripe availability. A 60-second Stripe timeout blocks the Order request thread. Cascading failures possible.","alternatives_considered":["Fully async payment with polling/webhooks — better resilience but adds UX complexity (pending states, webhook reconciliation)","Saga with compensation — appropriate if payment is one step in a multi-step workflow, overkill for two-service chain"]},{"decision":"Replace fire-and-forget with transactional outbox for notifications","gains":"Zero message loss. At-least-once delivery guarantee. Notifications survive Order Service restarts and Notification Service outages.","sacrifices":"Added complexity: outbox table, poller/CDC process, idempotent consumers. Slightly higher latency for notification dispatch.","alternatives_considered":["Message broker (RabbitMQ/SQS) — reliable but adds infrastructure dependency","Keep fire-and-forget with reconciliation job — simpler but delayed detection of lost notifications"]},{"decision":"Circuit breaker on Payment Service → Stripe","gains":"Fail fast during Stripe outages instead of accumulating blocked threads. Protects Payment Service from thread pool exhaustion. Gives Order Service a fast 'payment unavailable' signal.","sacrifices":"During open-circuit window, all payment attempts are rejected even if Stripe recovers. Half-open probing adds complexity.","alternatives_considered":["Only timeouts without circuit breaker — simpler but doesn't prevent thread pool exhaustion during prolonged outages","Bulkhead isolation — complements circuit breaker but doesn't replace it"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment gateway abstraction","evolution_path":"The Anti-Corruption Layer in Payment Service should abstract Stripe behind a PaymentGateway interface. When you need to add Adyen or PayPal, implement a new adapter without changing Order Service or Payment domain logic."},{"area":"Notification channel expansion","evolution_path":"With the outbox pattern, adding SMS/push notifications means adding new consumers of the same outbox events. The Order Service doesn't change."},{"area":"Async payment migration","evolution_path":"If payment latency becomes problematic, the synchronous call can be replaced with an async command + webhook pattern. The idempotency keys and payment state machine already support this — the Order aggregate just needs a 'payment_pending' state."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Synchronous Order→Payment coupling","reason":"This is intentionally rigid. Payment must succeed before an order is confirmed. Relaxing this requires accepting eventual consistency in the order lifecycle, which has significant UX and business logic implications. Keep this rigid until there is a compelling reason to change."},{"area":"Idempotency key contract","reason":"Once established, the idempotency key format and semantics cannot change without coordinating all producers and consumers simultaneously. Lock this contract early and version it."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Cascading failure: Stripe slowdown causes Payment Service thread pool exhaustion, which causes Order Service thread pool exhaustion, which causes full system unavailability","severity":"critical","mitigation":"1) Set aggressive timeouts: Order→Payment 5s, Payment→Stripe 10s. Total budget: 15s max. 2) Circuit breaker on Payment→Stripe (open after 5 failures in 30s, half-open probe every 15s). 3) Bulkhead: isolate payment HTTP client thread pool from other Payment Service operations."},{"risk":"Silent notification loss: Notification Service is down or Order Service crashes after payment but before firing the HTTP POST — customer pays but never receives confirmation email","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Replace fire-and-forget with transactional outbox pattern. Order Service writes a notification_outbox row in the same DB transaction that marks the order as paid. A poller or CDC process delivers to Notification Service with at-least-once semantics. Notification Service deduplicates by order_id."},{"risk":"Duplicate payment charges: Order Service retries after a timeout, but the first Stripe charge actually succeeded","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Idempotency keys at every layer. Order Service generates a deterministic idempotency_key (e.g., order_id + attempt_number) and passes it through Payment Service to Stripe. Stripe's native idempotency ensures at-most-once charging. Payment Service also stores idempotency_key→result for local deduplication."},{"risk":"Timeout budget misalignment: Order Service timeout is shorter than Payment Service timeout on Stripe, causing Order to give up while Payment still processes — resulting in a charged payment with a failed order","severity":"high","mitigation":"Enforce timeout budget hierarchy: Order→Payment timeout must be > Payment→Stripe timeout + processing overhead. Example: Stripe timeout 8s, Payment processing 1s, Order→Payment timeout 12s. Document and enforce this invariant in configuration."},{"risk":"Retry storm amplification: Order Service retries to Payment Service, which retries to Stripe, creating multiplicative load (e.g., 3 × 3 = 9 Stripe calls per original request)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Retry only at the outermost layer (Order Service). Payment Service does NOT retry to Stripe — it returns the failure immediately. If Payment Service must retry internally, Order Service must not. Never retry at more than one layer in a synchronous chain."},{"risk":"Stripe rate limiting: Burst traffic or retry storms trigger Stripe's rate limiter (429), causing widespread payment failures","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Client-side rate limiter in Payment Service (token bucket). Respect Stripe's Retry-After header. Circuit breaker will also help by shedding load during rate-limited periods."},{"risk":"Partial failure observability gap: Without distributed tracing, diagnosing whether a failure occurred at Order, Payment, or Stripe level requires correlating logs across three systems manually","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Propagate a correlation_id (trace_id) from Order Service through all downstream calls. Structured logging with correlation_id at every service boundary. Consider OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (immediate, critical): Add idempotency keys end-to-end and set explicit timeout budgets. Phase 2 (next sprint): Add circuit breaker on Payment→Stripe (use a proven library like resilience4j, Polly, or equivalent). Phase 3 (same sprint): Replace fire-and-forget notifications with transactional outbox. Phase 4 (hardening): Add bulkhead isolation, distributed tracing, and a reconciliation job that detects order-payment-notification state mismatches.","files_to_create_or_modify":["order-service/src/clients/PaymentClient — add timeout config (5s connect, 12s read), retry policy (2 retries, exponential backoff, only on 503/timeout), idempotency key generation","payment-service/src/clients/StripeGatewayClient — add circuit breaker (5 failures → open, 15s half-open probe), timeout (3s connect, 8s read), NO retries (leave that to Order Service layer)","payment-service/src/domain/PaymentService — add idempotency check before calling gateway, store idempotency_key→result mapping","order-service/src/domain/OrderService — write notification_outbox row in same transaction as order status update, remove direct HTTP call to Notification Service","order-service/src/infrastructure/OutboxPoller — poll notification_outbox table, POST to Notification Service, mark as delivered on 2xx, retry with backoff on failure","notification-service/src/api/NotificationController — add idempotent consumer logic (deduplicate by order_id, return 200 on duplicate)","shared/config/timeout-budget.yaml — document and enforce timeout hierarchy as configuration"],"patterns_to_follow":"Circuit Breaker (Stripe integration), Retry with Idempotency Key (Order→Payment), Transactional Outbox (notifications), Timeout Budget Hierarchy (cascading timeouts), Bulkhead Isolation (thread pool per downstream), Anti-Corruption Layer (Stripe abstraction), Correlation ID propagation (observability)","pitfalls_to_avoid":["NEVER retry at multiple layers in a synchronous call chain — pick one layer","NEVER retry non-idempotent operations without an idempotency key","Do NOT set inner timeout ≥ outer timeout — this causes the caller to give up while the callee is still working","Do NOT use fire-and-forget for any operation that has business consequences (notifications affect customer trust)","Do NOT catch and swallow timeout exceptions — they indicate a payment may have succeeded and the order state needs investigation","Avoid circuit breaker with too-short half-open intervals — it will flap and never stabilize"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Failure mode enumeration — these are well-known distributed systems failure patterns","Circuit breaker and timeout budget patterns — industry standard, extensively documented","Transactional outbox for notifications — proven pattern for exactly this fire-and-forget anti-pattern","Idempotency key requirement — essential for any payment system with retries"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact timeout values — the recommended numbers (5s, 8s, 12s) are reasonable defaults but should be tuned based on observed P99 latencies in production","Whether the team's stack has mature circuit breaker libraries available — implementation effort varies significantly by language/framework"],"assumptions":["Order Service and Payment Service each have their own database (not shared)","Stripe is the only payment gateway currently (no multi-gateway routing needed yet)","Order confirmation email is business-critical (customers expect it; silent loss is unacceptable)","The system runs on infrastructure where thread pool exhaustion is a realistic concern (e.g., thread-per-request model, not pure async/event-loop)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"This architecture has three critical integration points with distinct failure characteristics. The synchronous chain from Order→Payment→Stripe creates a cascading failure risk where any downstream outage blocks order processing entirely. The fire-and-forget notification call silently loses emails with no recovery path. The design needs circuit breakers, retries with idempotency, a transactional outbox for notifications, and timeout budgets to prevent cascade amplification."
